Fig. 6: Measured and commanded interaction wrenches on the payload for two robot embodiments in simulation.

Fig. 2: Overview of the partner-agnostic intent-aware cooperative transport framework. The system contains privileged training in simulation and lightweight real-world deployment via a hierarchical controller: (A) Intent (Force/Torque) Generator: In simulation, random forces and torques are applied to the payload to simulate partner guidance, producing diverse intent signals. (B) High-Level Controller: A HL policy maps intent to base commands and arm joint actions. The HL policy is trained with teacher-student scheme, where a teacher (B1) uses wrench observations, and a deployable student (B2) relies on proprioceptive histories with an intent estimator network to infer interaction wrench; KL regularization (B3) distills the teacher’s action distribution into the student. (C) Low-Level Controller: A terrain-robust locomotion backbone tracks the HL planar commands and outputs leg actions for stable loco-manipulation under load. (D) Evaluation: The framework is developed in simulation (D1) and transferred to real-world deployment (D2), where it operates without end-effector FT sensing or payload tracking.

Fig. 3: Overview of partner-agnostic intent-aware cooperative transport results. The proposed intent-aware framework enables stable cooperative transport across diverse terrains, payloads, partners, robot teams, and robot embodiments.
